common_name: Malabar Chestnut (Money Tree, Guiana Chestnut, Saba Nut)

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees after all frost danger has passed in moist, well-drained, fertile soil; in zone 9a protect from frost — young trees are killed by hard freezes, though established trees may resprout from the base after a light freeze. Full sun to partial shade; tolerates wet or seasonally flooded soils well, which makes it valuable near ponds or in low spots where other trees struggle.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained to seasonally wet soil in partial shade to full sun; Malabar chestnut is best known to most people as the ornamental 'money tree' with braided trunks sold as a houseplant across the world, but it is also a genuine food plant producing 12-inch oval pods filled with 20–25 large, edible seeds that taste remarkably like chestnuts or roasted peanuts when cooked — the seeds can be roasted, boiled, or ground into flour, and the young leaves and flowers are edible as vegetables. As a landscape tree in zone 9b–12 it can reach 30–40 feet in the ground with a spreading tropical canopy, but is easy to keep pruned to 10–15 feet. The large, palmate leaves and buttressed trunk give it outstanding ornamental character. Native to the seasonally flooded forests of Central and South America, it tolerates both dry periods and extended flooding — a rare combination that makes it valuable for challenging low-lying sites.
sources: Morton, J. 1987. Fruits of Warm Climates

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in water or plant in boggy, consistently wet soil after all frost danger; rice paddy herb performs best in wet to aquatic conditions — it thrives planted at the edge of ponds, in water gardens, in consistently wet raised beds, or in containers sitting in trays of water. In zone 9–10 it is a true perennial; in colder zones grow as an annual in a sunny spot with moist soil.

directions: Root stem cuttings in water or wet soil in partial shade to full sun; rice paddy herb is the fresh herb most responsible for the distinctive, complex aroma of Vietnamese pho and many other Southeast Asian soups — the crushed leaves have a remarkably layered fragrance combining cumin, rice, lemon, and a unique floral quality that is difficult to describe but instantly recognizable in a bowl of authentic pho. It is used fresh as a tableside herb for hot soups and spring rolls in Vietnamese cuisine, where it is called rau om. The plant grows naturally in rice paddies and flooded fields throughout tropical Asia, requiring consistently wet to aquatic conditions — it will not thrive in typical garden soil that dries between waterings. Plant at pond margins, in water gardens, or in containers kept sitting in 1–2 inches of water for reliable production. In zone 9–10 it persists as a perennial through mild winters; harvest regularly by pinching stems to keep plants bushy and productive.
sources: Asian Vegetable Research and Development Center (AVRDC)
Pham, H.H. Cay Co Vietnam (An Illustrated Flora of Vietnam)

planting.note: Plant corm divisions or cormels in well-drained, humus-rich, fertile soil in late spring once soil is thoroughly warm; suran produces one enormous leaf (a deeply divided umbrella-like structure on a single mottled petiole) per season and then dies back to the corm in autumn. In zone 9 leave corms in the ground with mulch protection, or dig and store above freezing. Partial shade is tolerated well and preferred in zone 9+ summers.

directions: Plant corm sections with at least one bud 4–6 inches deep in loose, fertile, humus-rich, well-drained soil; suran is a major root crop of South and Southeast Asia — a different species from konjac (Amorphophallus konjac) and much more important as a direct food source, producing starchy corms that can weigh 2–5 kg and are used extensively in Indian cooking as suran or oal, in Indonesian cooking as iles-iles, and across tropical Asia in curries, stews, and chips. The corm must be cooked before eating to neutralize calcium oxalate crystals that cause irritation when raw — boiling, roasting, or frying renders the corm safe and gives a texture similar to taro or potato with a mildly earthy flavor that absorbs spices beautifully. The plant emerges in spring as a single dramatic mottled petiole supporting an enormous, deeply divided leaf canopy 3–5 feet wide — extraordinary as a tropical foliage accent in the garden. Corms multiply readily and can be divided each year, building a productive patch.
sources: Asian Vegetable Research and Development Center (AVRDC)
van Wyk, B. Food Plants of the World

common_name: Bat Flower (Black Bat Flower, Cat's Whiskers)

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in deep shade to partial shade; bat flower is one of the few tropical ornamentals that genuinely prefers deep shade — direct sun scorches the leaves rapidly. In zone 9 it needs frost protection; grow in a container that can be brought indoors, or mulch heavily and hope for mild winters. High humidity is essential.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in deep to partial shade with high humidity; bat flower produces one of the most extraordinary flowers in the plant kingdom — the inflorescence consists of two large, dark purple-black wing-like bracts (resembling a bat in flight), a cluster of maroon-purple flowers in the center, and up to 28 long, thread-like purple whiskers (bracteoles) hanging down 12–28 inches, giving the flower a ghostly, otherworldly appearance. Native to the forest floor of tropical Southeast Asia from southern China to Malaysia, it requires warm, humid, shaded conditions and is intolerant of direct sun, drought, or temperature below 50°F. In zone 10–12 it is a reliable outdoor perennial shade plant; in zone 9 it is best grown in a container as a specimen that can be overwintered inside. The large, glossy leaves are attractive year-round even when not in flower, and the plants bloom from late spring through autumn.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; sandankwa viburnum is the workhorse hedge viburnum of the Gulf Coast and central Florida, thriving where northern viburnums struggle in summer heat and humidity. Avoid planting in poorly-drained sites; it tolerates light drought once established but will not stand waterlogged roots.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; sandankwa viburnum is the most widely used broadleaf evergreen hedge and screening shrub in Gulf Coast and central Florida landscapes — an easy-growing, tough, relatively drought-tolerant Japanese species that forms a dense, 5–8 foot mound of glossy, dark green, oval leaves and produces clusters of small, fragrant white to pale pink flowers in winter and early spring when few other shrubs bloom. The flowers are followed by red fruits that mature to black, attracting birds. It tolerates partial shade well and performs reliably in the heat and humidity that limits many northern shrubs in zone 8b–10 gardens. Widely used for foundation planting, privacy hedges, and screening in Florida and along the Gulf Coast; easy to shear to a formal shape or allow to grow naturally as an informal screen.

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in fall in well-drained, sandy to loamy, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun to partial shade; simpson stopper is native to the coastal hammocks and pinelands of South Florida and the Keys and is one of the most wildlife-valuable native shrubs for zone 9b–11 gardens. Tolerates salt spray and brief flooding; very drought-tolerant once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun to partial shade; simpson stopper is a Florida-native small tree or large shrub with flaking cinnamon-colored bark, glossy dark green oval leaves, and small white flowers with prominent stamens that have a pleasant spicy fragrance — the flowers are followed by small, orange to red, berry-like fruits that are edible and have a pleasantly spicy, aromatic flavor used for wildlife food, flavoring, and nibbling fresh. The peeling bark is one of the most distinctive features, revealing warm salmon-cinnamon tones beneath. Native to the coastal hammocks, pine rocklands, and tropical hardwood hammocks of South Florida and the Florida Keys, it is exceptionally well-adapted to the calcareous, well-drained soils of peninsular Florida and tolerates salt spray, drought, and partial shade. An important nectar source for butterflies and the berries are eaten by many species of birds and wildlife, making it an outstanding Florida native wildlife plant for landscapes in zone 9b–11.
sources: Wunderlin, R. and Hansen, B. Flora of Florida

planting.note: Plant container-grown or rooted cuttings after last frost in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; provide a sturdy fence, trellis, or arbor as support. Garlic vine blooms most prolifically in fall and again in spring with intermittent bloom throughout the warm season; it flowers better in full sun but tolerates partial shade. Frost damages foliage in zone 9 but established plants typically resprout from the base.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade with sturdy support for the vigorous vines; garlic vine (Mansoa alliacea) is a striking tropical vine from the Amazon basin producing clusters of lavender-purple to purple-pink, trumpet-shaped bignonia-family flowers in successive waves through the warm season, most heavily in fall and spring. Its common name comes from the pronounced garlic odor of the crushed leaves, stems, and flowers — a distinctive fragrance that has led to its use in traditional medicine in South America. The three-part leaves and decorative clusters of flowers make it an attractive flowering vine for tropical and subtropical screens and arbors; the vines climb vigorously and can cover a large fence or pergola quickly. It flowers most prolifically in full sun but tolerates partial shade and performs well in the humid heat of Gulf Coast zone 9–10 gardens. The unusual garlic fragrance is surprising and memorable in the garden.
sources: Lorenzi, H. and Matos, F.J. Plantas Medicinais no Brasil

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm, well-drained soil; papaloquelite self-seeds readily in zone 9–11 and may naturalize in warm gardens. In Mexico it is often grown with corn, beans, and squash in traditional milpa gardens, tolerating the partial shade of taller corn plants.

directions: Sow seeds on the soil surface after last frost in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; papaloquelite is a traditional Mesoamerican culinary herb used throughout Mexico, Central America, and Bolivia with a distinctive aroma that combines cilantro, rue, and a strong, almost petroleum-like note that is at once exotic and intensely savory — the flavor used extensively in Mexican salsas, pozole, tacos de canasta, and Oaxacan cooking as a bolder, heat-stable alternative to cilantro. Unlike cilantro, which wilts when heated, papaloquelite retains flavor when briefly cooked and is used as a fresh herb added at the end of cooking or stirred into hot soups tableside. The oval, glandular-dotted leaves on the blue-green stems are harvested by pinching stem tips regularly. In zone 9 it grows as a warm-season annual that self-seeds freely in favorable sites; drought-tolerant once established, it thrives in the heat that limits cilantro production in Gulf Coast summers, making it a superb cilantro substitute for tropical-climate gardeners.
sources: Bye, R. and Linares, E. Ethnobotany of Economic Plants of Mexico

common_name: Tropical Turk's Cap (Sleeping Hibiscus, Cardinal's Hat)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or divisions in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun to heavy partial shade; tropical Turk's cap is more frost-tender than the native Malvaviscus arboreus — in zone 9a it needs frost protection or may die back to the roots; in zone 9b–12 it is a true perennial shrub. Like M. arboreus, it is a hummingbird magnet.

directions: Root stem cuttings in moist sand or plant divisions in well-drained soil in partial to full shade; tropical Turk's cap is a Mexican species closely related to the native Turk's cap (Malvaviscus arboreus) but distinctly different — producing pendulous, elongated, deep red, twisted flowers 2–3 inches long that hang downward on long stalks rather than the upright flower of M. arboreus, giving it a more dramatic, drooping floral display. Like its relative, the flowers never fully open — they remain twisted into a tube, accessible only to hummingbirds whose long bills can reach the nectar at the base. Tropical Turk's cap is a large, fast-growing shrub reaching 6–10 feet with large, tropical-looking leaves and year-round bloom in frost-free areas, making it one of the most reliably productive hummingbird plants for zone 9b–12 gardens. It tolerates significant shade and grows vigorously under tree canopies where other flowering shrubs struggle. More frost-tender than the native species, requiring protection or regrowth from roots in zone 9a.
sources: University of Texas at Austin Native Plant Collection
